title: Melatonin Dosage Calculator
tagline: Calculate an appropriate melatonin starting dose based on body weight, age, and your specific sleep-related purpose.
intro: Enter your body weight, age, and the reason you are considering melatonin. The calculator applies evidence-informed weight-based dosing guidelines to suggest a reasonable starting dose.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid body weight and age greater than zero.

about.heading: About the melatonin dosage calculator
about.body:
Melatonin is a naturally occurring hormone produced by the pineal gland in response to darkness. It plays a central role in regulating the body's circadian rhythm — the roughly 24-hour internal clock that governs sleep and wakefulness, hormone release, body temperature, and many other physiological processes. As a supplement, melatonin is widely used to help people fall asleep faster, adjust to new time zones, adapt to rotating shift schedules, or simply promote a sense of calm relaxation before bed.

Unlike many sleep aids, melatonin is not a sedative. It works by signalling to the brain that darkness has arrived and that it is time to prepare for sleep, which is why timing is often as important as dose. Taking melatonin 30–60 minutes before a desired bedtime is generally recommended for most sleep-onset purposes. For jet lag, strategically timed doses at the destination's local bedtime can accelerate circadian re-entrainment by one to two days.

Dosing recommendations have evolved considerably over the past decade. Early clinical studies often used doses of 5–10 mg, but more recent research suggests that much smaller doses — in the range of 0.1–0.5 mg — can be equally effective for many individuals while causing fewer next-day side effects such as grogginess or daytime sleepiness. This calculator uses a weight-based approach to estimate a starting dose, because body weight is one of the factors that influences how quickly melatonin is metabolised. Adults metabolise melatonin differently than children, which is reflected in the separate dosing factors applied.

The purpose multiplier accounts for the fact that different applications call for different pharmacological profiles. Jet lag recovery, for example, benefits from a slightly lower dose that avoids excessive daytime sedation during travel. Shift work adjustment, by contrast, may warrant a marginally higher dose to overcome stronger circadian resistance when sleeping at non-traditional hours. General relaxation without a primary sleep disorder is best served by the lowest effective dose.

It is important to understand that this calculator provides a suggested starting dose only, not a prescription. Individual responses to melatonin vary substantially due to differences in enzyme activity (primarily CYP1A2), age-related changes in pineal gland output, medication interactions, and underlying health conditions. Always start at the lowest calculated dose, and only increase gradually if the initial dose is insufficient. Children and adolescents should use melatonin only under medical supervision. Pregnant or breastfeeding individuals should consult a healthcare provider before use. Melatonin may interact with anticoagulants, immunosuppressants, and certain antidepressants.

examples.heading: Melatonin dosage examples
examples.intro: Sample calculations showing how weight, age, and purpose influence the recommended starting dose.

items.note: 70 × 0.05 = 3.5 mg base. Purpose multiplier 1.0 applies, giving 3.5 mg (high-dose classification). Consider starting with a lower dose and titrating up based on response.
85 × 0.05 = 4.25 mg base, clamped to 4.25 mg. Jet-lag multiplier of 0.8 applied: 4.25 × 0.8 = 3.4 mg. A lower dose is preferable during travel to minimise daytime drowsiness.
35 × 0.025 = 0.875 mg base, rounded to 0.9 mg. Children use a lower weight factor. Paediatric use should always be supervised by a doctor.
60 × 0.05 = 3.0 mg base. Shift-work multiplier of 1.2 gives 3.6 mg. High-dose classification; starting low and titrating up is recommended.

howto.heading: How to use the melatonin dosage calculator
steps: Enter your body weight and select the unit (kg or lbs). The calculator automatically converts pounds to kilograms for the calculation.
Enter your age in years. Children (under 18) receive a lower weight-based dosing factor than adults.
Select your purpose: Sleep Optimization, Jet Lag Recovery, Shift Work Adjustment, or General Relaxation. Each purpose applies a different multiplier.
Click Calculate Dose to see your suggested starting dose in milligrams along with a low/standard/high classification.
Treat the result as a starting point. Begin at the lowest suggested dose and adjust gradually based on your individual response, consulting a healthcare provider if needed.

faq.heading: Melatonin dosage FAQ
faq.q1: What is the lowest effective melatonin dose?
faq.q1_a: Research suggests that doses as low as 0.1–0.3 mg can shift the circadian clock and improve sleep onset in many adults. Higher doses do not necessarily produce stronger effects; they primarily extend the duration of elevated melatonin in the blood, which can cause next-day grogginess.
faq.q2_a: Short-term use of low-dose melatonin appears safe for children with sleep disorders, particularly those associated with neurodevelopmental conditions, when used under medical guidance. Long-term safety data in children is limited, and over-the-counter use should always be discussed with a paediatrician first.
faq.q3: When should I take melatonin for jet lag?
faq.q3_a: For eastward travel, take melatonin at the destination's target bedtime starting on the day of arrival. For westward travel, take it just before sleep at the new local bedtime. Taking it too early in the evening can advance your clock in the wrong direction.
faq.q4: Can I take melatonin every night long-term?
faq.q4_a: Melatonin is generally considered safe for short- to medium-term use (weeks to months). Long-term nightly use is less well-studied. Some experts suggest using the lowest effective dose intermittently rather than nightly to preserve the brain's sensitivity to endogenous melatonin signals.
faq.q5_a: Yes. Melatonin can interact with blood thinners (warfarin), immunosuppressants, certain antidepressants (SSRIs, fluvoxamine in particular), and diabetes medications. Fluvoxamine dramatically increases melatonin blood levels. Always disclose melatonin use to your prescribing physician.
